speaker: interviewer
text: --the war, did you talk about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I talk about it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: To whom?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it to people--

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, are you-- you think-- are you asking if I was interviewed, or just between our friends?

speaker: interviewer
text: Between friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Between friends? Yeah, sure. In fact, whenever you meet each other, you talk-- it always comes out. We talk about our experiences. Yesterday we went for a coffee, talk about. No matter what you talk about, you're winding down, talking about it, what-- how you lived through the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date, February 7, 1995. Survivor, Michael Kaplan. Interviewer, Marjorie Katz. City of Margate, Florida. Country, USA. Language, English. Tape one. Can you tell us your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael Kaplan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind spelling that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael, M-I-C-H-A-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Kaplan?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-P-L-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Micheul. How do you spell Micheul?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-C-H-U-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 12, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind spelling that city?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-L-I-S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: What big town was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Kalisz itself was a big town-- 100,000 population. Near Lodz, between Lodz and Poznan.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you tell me a little bit about your parents, brothers, sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we were a family of really-- I have to start. My father, when I was six or seven years old, he left the family, and he went on the way to Israel. And, and this was after World War I. He-- they used to-- they crossed the borders like we used to cross the border after World War II, in groups. He left the family because he couldn't make a living. So they crossed the border. With the group, he crossed the border to Romania. And in Romania, they caught him on the border. They put him in jail. And in jail was a typhus epidemic at that time, so he died. And he's buried at Iasi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember much of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your mother. What was her typical day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, like a housewife, left without a husband, and we were youngsters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had very tough times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, you see, there was no Social Security, no welfare, and no organizations that will support a family in this case, you know. And we were all youngsters and we weren't yet-- we didn't yet work. Except my oldest brother, he was inducted in the Polish Army. So we were left just-- my wife-- my mother was left just with five kids. And even that in Europe, if you go in apprenticeship, you go for three years. And the three years, you don't get paid. You just work for, for-- if you work for food, that's fine. But you just work 8-10 hours a day, and that's it. So we-- many, many times, I was really hungry-- the whole family, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did the family get support? How did the family get money then?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, my grandfather-- my mother comes from bakers. My grandfather was a baker. So he lived about 10, 12 kilometers away from, from our town. So from time to time, he used to come to town, and he brought some food, flour, or bread or something like that. But my mother never complained. Not, not at-- not to my knowledge, that she complained that we are hungry, and we need support. That's a-- it's Jewish nature not, not to-- not, not to show that you are-- you are in a bad shape, that you're going hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So really, all of you had a pretty struggling early life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the Jewish community in which you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the population was 100,000. The Jewish population-- was about 30,000 Jewish people. And you know, the Jewish people, their trades were shoes-- a shoemaker, a tailor, a carpenter, a barber, these kind of things. A Jew could not get a, a job in the Polish government. That's, that's out of the question. So roofers-- so one, one dealt with the other. And that's how we made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish organizations? If there were 30,000 people-- a cheder, shuls, other Jewish institutions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had one big shul, the main shul. Then the German Jews had their own shul, you know. Because they kept themselves separate, because they consider themselves a little higher class, more intelligent, more civilized, so to speak. But the rest of us, we had one big shul which was supported by the kehilla. Kehilla was a committee that was elected by the Jewish population, around the Jewish life, so to speak. And they had to [INAUDIBLE] position to cheders, cheder. You know what a cheder is. And this was-- the kehilla supported these cheders. And we had, oh, let's say-- from all the extreme right to the extreme left Jewish organizations. You have like Betar and the, the policy on the left and right. And then you have the Hashomer Hatzair, Gordonia, all, all, all these kind of organizations. It was a very-- it was a very robust life in, in, in, in Kalisz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother or your brothers, or anybody in the family, belong to any political organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, mine three brothers belonged to the [INAUDIBLE] left-- that's the [INAUDIBLE] part of it. [INAUDIBLE] is a [INAUDIBLE], this organization. I myself was at that time 17-- 16, 17 years, I belonged to a Hashomer Hatzair. And my sister belonged to a Hanoar Hatzioni. All, all of us belonged to Zionist organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you had these Zionist meetings, did you have any office in the organization? You were just a regular member?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were members, you know. What are you calling by office?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you an officer, like the secretary of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, if I was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a-- yes. Were you a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was too young. Mine-- I belonged to Hatshomer Hatzair and my [INAUDIBLE] was Josef Kaplan. If you heard about it, he was a leader in the Warsaw ghetto in the culture department. And there we, we had two other, other friends-- [INAUDIBLE] and Zorach Zilberman, and another girl that died in the uprising in, in Vilna ghetto. So, you know, these, these other people grew up out, out of these organizations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did these organizations have pamphlets or leaflets, or did you have any other type of magazines, books around your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Books we, we, we got from-- there was a Jewish library. So you go there and, and, and borrow books. And, we used to go-- you know, in summertime, used to go for camp, so to speak. But it was not a-- the way a camp is in the United States. We just got together. We slept together in the-- in the-- int the haystack, so to speak. And that's how we spent a week or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother communicate by writing or any other method with people outside of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, because we didn't have nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really didn't have any letters or any contact with people outside of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing-- my sister left Poland just with the last boat that left Constanta from Romania in-- she, she, she went illegally. She got a certificate. We, we, we, we-- my mother got some-- somehow she got money to-- scraped up money. And we bought a certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went-- left Poland illegally into Romania, into Constanta. In Constanta, she's supposed to get the boat. She went on the boat there, and, and-- but she didn't have no entry visa into Israel, like a certificate we used to get.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, the English government used to give out certificates for immigration. That's how they controlled the, the immigration to Israel in those days. This is I'm talking about 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the middle of the ocean, they hang a net to cover the boat and they just drove it into Haifa. And that's-- she got off the boat, and she remained. And they got-- you know, they got-- they got her a certificate over there. She got, got, got off the boat. And since then, we used to get letters from, from our sister from Israel to Europe. Always we had no other correspondence. Do you remember what year it was that your sister got to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39. She left in-- before-- in-- before the war, as I told you. The last boat had left Constanta. That's-- she went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep track of the Jewish calendar growing up? Did you have a Jewish calendar, or was it through the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't much-- too much into it. I mean, you're talking about a Hebrew calendar? I knew that Shavuot is coming, Sukkot, or Pesach is coming. Pesach was a big deal, because you used to take out the [INAUDIBLE], you clean, and wash, and everything. So this-- But all the holidays, we didn't-- we didn't really-- we had the matzahs, seder. A seder without the father. So the-- these, these holidays we observed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What provisions were made for observances of Jewish law that were connected with bar mitzvahs, or when somebody died, Jewish weddings? Did you have a bar mitzvah, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a bar mitzvah. I just was told that I-- I'm 12 years old. And I remember mine oldest brother's girlfriend-- she was at that time a girlfriend-- she bought me a watch for my bar mitzvah. That's all I got.

speaker: interviewer
text: But if somebody died, the kehilla had a group. These-- what they call them? [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: They took care of the dead people. They washed them, cleaned them, and were taken out to the cemetery. This was organized. And then we had the [INAUDIBLE], which were also included in, in the-- in the-- in the care of the-- of the Jewish committee of the kehilla.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family, would you consider them traditional Jews, observant Jews, non-observant Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own mother was observant Jew, but not us. I used to argue with my mother. You believe in God? You wnat-- you want to play? Fine, but I don't believe in it. So, you know, somehow we made a-- we made-- she, she did what she, she supposed-- she was brought up to do. And I did that I-- You see, what happened, really, my father was a religious man, very religious. And if father would have lived, I would have been probably a pious Jew, a religious Jew. But since there, there was no father in the house, everybody did, did-- went his own, own, own ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were outside the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know any other languages at that time, growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you like as a child? Were you a happy child? Were you a sullen child because you were a-- without a father? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of I remember from my childhood is I was hungry. Didn't have enough bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a seder--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And having a seder, in particular, without a father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memorable Jewish occasions, whether it was a Shabbat or a Passover, or a Hanukkah? Could you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Passover-- we, we, we, we, we had the seder, you know. That's what it is, but it a short-- a short-- not like a traditional seder, like other people had in those days. Short seder, they just keep up the tradition. We ate matzohs and those kind of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of schooling did you have? Did you go to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school four years. And there, it was like-- in the public school, they used to give us-- since I was hungry. I was going around hung-- I was hungry-- so in the public school, when you went in the morning, they gave you a glass of milk and a roll of bread. But I got beaten up every day because I was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the-- it was the, the, the, the question, do you go to school or you don't go to school? If you go to school, you get the bread and milk. And if you don't go, you go hungry. So I had no choice. I had to go to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: But every day in school was, was a, a-- was a-- I hate it, because you were beaten up. And if you went to, to the-- to the principal, he said, you don't like it, go to Israel. I would gladly go to Israel, but who, who, who-- the English didn't let us in. Oh-- so we-- Eventually when I grow old-- I got older, in the same house where we lived, a Jewish guy had a, a,a factory of the cigarette pieces. Not with-- without, without the tobacco. Later on, you had to stick it, push in the tobacco. So he employed me half a day, so I made 50 groszy, like $0.50. I made some money over the week, so mother had money to buy bread. And then later on I-- when I was a, a-- older, I start to go in an apprenticeship to, to be a-- not a shoemaker. See, in Europe, they upper part from the-- from the shoes was one, one operation, and the lower part of the shoe, somebody else made it. So this-- the lower part was a shoemaker. The upper part was a [NON-ENGLISH], a upper part maker. So I was working in the-- in that place learning the trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you had all this antisemitism directed at you at school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also have it at work?

speaker: interviewee
text: At work, no. All this war, the cottage industry, everybody, you know, had one or two people working, all Jewish people. This was all in Jewish hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel that there was any special way they identified you as being a Jew when you were in public school, or did they just knew who the Jews in the city were?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, the Polish people-- this is from-- it's from generations back, they could smell a Jew. They could smell a Jew. We had this experience with them when, when the Germans invaded Poland. First of all, the name. And then in the school, when, when, when they got up every day for the prayer, the Jews didn't get up. So I got beaten for why didn't I get up, help them with the prayer every day. I got beaten for this. So this identify you, you already that you are Jewish. Boy or girls, that doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe your home? What kind of building it was? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a-- it was a three-story building. So on the-- on the ground floor was a factory making-- what do you call it? Bags. Like you go to, to Publix, you have a shopping-- they put it in bags. It was a factory making bags. On the first-- On the second floor was the guy that I worked-- we used to work for him, Mr. Kaufman. And on the third floor already, people lived. And we worked a floor above-- we lived on floor above.

speaker: interviewer
text: So was this an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: An apartment. It was from factories made into-- made to-- made into apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any people live with you other than members of the family, a grandparent or a boarder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. Only, only the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just the family. Did anybody in the family have any special hobbies or play an instrument, piano? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- our hobby was reading, which gave us something. And then we played chess. It was cheap, cheap toy. A cheap way to entertain yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you remember what types of things you liked to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read-- it was-- in those days, this was a dream. I remember A U-boat went over the North Pole underneath. And all this kind of books, I liked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Science fiction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like adventure, but has something to do with possible things that could happen in life. You know, and we lived through that. Today you have submarines going through all over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to-- used to-- Saturdays, we used to go on the stadium in, in-- we were sport-- running and jumping in the sport-- how should I say? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It used to be a sport club, Maccabi. We belonged there. So every Saturday, we went, and we had this exercise. That-- this was our entertainment, too, partially. And from time to time, the Jewish people themselves perform theatre, which I couldn't go-- get in, so I sneaked in. When everybody was in, they closed the doors. I sneaked in to see the show. That's what it was. When did you first become aware that-- the rise of Nazism and have, perhaps, fear over your future?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, this was in 19-- when Hitler came to power. He came to power in 1933. In 1937, I think it was, he-- there were substantial Polish Jews that lived in Germany. So he took these Jews and dropped them over the border into Poland. We read in the-- we read in the papers that it was going on in Germany when the Nazis came to power. But, you know, paper. But since they dropped these people over the border into Poland, and they start coming to our town, the kehilla, the Jewish committee, start to settle them, gave them apartments and buy them furniture, all these things. And then we heard directly from them. Then we got the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was this perceived by the community? Did-- there were 30,000 Jews in your town. Did you believe the things that were happening, or did you more or less pooh-pooh them? What was the perception?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, Jewish history is full of programs, full of catastrophes. You take-- go back to the Spanish Inquisition. You go back when the Romans-- when the Romans conquer Israel, they disperse the Jews. The Jews came into Rome and, and, and, and lived in ghettos. So all through our history we used to. But we, we never thought that it would come to a point that Jewish-- taking people in concentration camps. No. We thought, all right, he's crazy. So he dropped all-- sent back the Polish citizens back to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family ever make any attempts to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all-- as I told you, my sister left. We-- my oldest brother tried to go to, to Stock-- to Sweden. But he didn't have the money to go to Sweden. He wanted to go to Sweden. And then with Sweden, he thinks that he go to, to the United States, but there was no way. Even if you had the money, you couldn't. It, it-- nobody would give you a visa. The Germans Jews, I heard, and I know from way back, somehow-- because probably there, there were rich people that had the money so they could buy themselves a visa, or deposit some money so that they were allowed to come in to America, to, to Argentina, to other countries. But for the average Jewish people in Poland, poor people, they had nowhere to go, not a prayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you September 1, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about that day when Germany invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in, in 1939 Kalisz-- when the Germans invaded Poland-- this was on a Friday-- Saturday afternoon we had to really leave the town because the Germans were already about 5, 3 kilometers away from town. And we knew what we had to expect from the Poles-- Poles and Germans. Because when Poland was, was born, after World War I, Poland got a, a part of Germany in-- in-- incorporated into Poland. So we had quite a big population of Germans living in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Poles we knew, we knew then what to expect from them. So we, we got-- prepared ourselves to leave-- to leave town, to run away. Because, you know, we, we, we knew already the Poles antisemites. The Germans, what we knew from the-- from what we were taught, from witnesses that came in from-- away from Germany-- so we couldn't expect any-- anything good from them. So we start running away. And we left town. But this I have to tell you, we had a rabbi there. So people went-- you know, usually the rabbi is the wise man. He give you good advice and everything. People run to him, went to him and asked, rabbi, what should we do? Should we run, or should we stay? He said, no, you go home, stay, and God will help you. This was in, in, in, in July-- no, in September, I think the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, a Jew can only do things after he see the first star. Then he can do things, pack or whatever. But this, this was 5, 6 o'clock. The Germans were already coming into town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think the rabbi did? He took a taxi. He left us high and dry. He ran away. He, he, he drove away. This is the rabbi. Do you remember the rabbi's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember now. I think [? Lipshitz ?] was it, another guy. Anyway, this was the fact. We, we-- he ran away. We were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so what do I do? I, I-- mine brothers had already girlfriends. They left with their girlfriends. The oldest brother was married already, so he left with his wife. And we figure like this. Mother-- my mother was what? 50 years old. Who would bother old women? So I took my old-- my younger brother. We put two breads in, in, in a-- in a sack, and we walked away from town. We walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there's another irony in life. While we walked, we walked about 50-40 kilometers from town and I was sitting on the side of the road and eating my bread. And there, I have to go back. I have to tell you this. This has to be for-- to history. People-- people will learn something. When we were hungry, when we were going hungry, my mother had a cousin there. He was a rich man, a very rich man. In those days he had already two houses in [INAUDIBLE] himself. He had a beautiful, big, big house in our town, and the house was covered with tiles. This was unique in those days. In his house lived the bishop. Imagine, in, in 1936 he, he brought in a, a, a, a decorator to decorate his, his apartment. So that's how rich he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, when we were going hungry, my, my, my mother-- I [INAUDIBLE]. That's why I can tell you this story. And she asked me, Jacob, could you help me? We're going hungry. When the kids start to grow up, I'll pay you back or whatever. So he said, no. I'm not-- I cannot be part of this. Go to my wife. So we went to his wife. She said, I'm help-- I'm helping only my family. So on the way home, my mother was crying, and I was crying, and we were going hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we escaped from Kalisz, and I had these two breads in, in my bag, and I was sitting on the, on the highway and eating the bread, he comes along. Oh, Michuel! Michuel! Oh, he-- oh. He was so happy to see me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what, what happened all of a sudden? There you didn't want to talk to me. You didn't want to help me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the bread. So he said, yeah. So So said, you know what. Give me a bread. I'll give you-- he has a whole bag of diamonds with, with, with jewelry and dollar pieces, you know, all these things he had there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll give you dollars. I'll give you jewelry. Give me a bread. I said, Jacob, what we going to do with this jewelry now? You can't buy bread for it. So I gave him half a bread. And I said, here's a half a bread. I don't need the jewelry. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: He still has a daughter survived. She lives in, in, in, in Brooklyn. From time to time, you know, we see each other. So she, she remembers. So that-- that's life, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we were running away because the Germans were coming into Kalisz. I went into Turek, and there I met my mother already. You know, you are so mixed up, so tense, that I did-- forgot to ask her, well, how did she get there? Probably she got there hitchhiked on, on, on, on a horse and buggy, something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we, we, we-- my brother left his wife there. And we went on the way to Warsaw. Because in Warsaw, they-- the Poles used to say, well, the Germans are so heavy. They have such heavy, heavy equipment that they'll never make it because it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Finish that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's-- the, the, the roads are dirty roads, and, and they get bogged down. This what they were trying to tell us. So we--

speaker: interviewer
text: We've got to do it.
